title: English A2 · Listening Lab
description: Everyday English: Puedo hablar de rutinas, planes, compras y lugares.

content_json.intro: A2 Listening: understand short conversations about shopping, food, directions, weather, plans, and simple past experiences. You will practice catching prices, times, and places, following simple announcements, and understanding the main point of everyday exchanges spoken clearly.
content_json.mission: Describe your daily routine in 5 short sentences.
content_json.grammar: Past simple, present continuous, comparatives, frequency adverbs.

vocabulary.example: I have an appointment at five.
I go to the grocery store on Fridays.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Good afternoon! Are you ready to order?

dialogue.speaker: Maria
dialogue.line: Yes, please. I'd like a cheese sandwich and an orange juice.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Maria
dialogue.line: Yes, how much is the chocolate cake?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: It's three fifty. It's the most popular cake on the menu.

dialogue.speaker: Maria
dialogue.line: Great, one piece of chocolate cake, please.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: So that's a cheese sandwich, an orange juice, and a chocolate cake.

dialogue.speaker: Maria
dialogue.line: That's right. Can I pay by card?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course. That's nine seventy-five in total.

dialogue.speaker: Maria
dialogue.line: Here you are. Thank you very much!

reading.text: Last Saturday, Daniel visited the new market in his neighborhood. He got up early, had a quick breakfast, and walked there with his sister Elena. First, they looked at the fruit stands and bought some apples and bananas because they were very cheap. Then, Daniel found a small bookshop inside the market and bought a book about animals for two dollars. After that, they were hungry, so they ordered two sandwiches and lemonade at a little café. The waiter was very friendly and recommended the chocolate cake, but it was too expensive. Finally, they took the bus home at five o'clock. It was a simple day, but Daniel says it was the best Saturday of the month.
questions: What is the main idea of the text?
Who went to the market with Daniel?
Why did they buy apples and bananas?
What did Daniel buy at the bookshop, and how much was it?
What did they order at the café?
Why didn't they eat the chocolate cake?
How and when did they go home?
Write three sentences about your last weekend using first, then, and finally.

Describe your daily routine in 5 short sentences.
Lee en voz alta: I usually...
¿Qué significa "Busy"?
Describe tu horario de mañana, tarde y noche usando "usually" y "last weekend".

tasks: Listen to a shopping dialogue and write down the prices you hear.
Describe your daily routine in six sentences using frequency adverbs.
Order a complete meal in a role-play restaurant conversation.
Read a menu and answer questions about dishes and prices.
Draw a simple map and give a partner directions to three places.
Write a postcard about a real or imaginary trip (60–80 words).
Tell the story of your last weekend using first, then, and finally.
Compare two cities or two shops using comparatives and superlatives.
Make plans for next Saturday with a partner using going to.
Interview a classmate about their favorite food and report the answers.
communicativeFunctions: Describe daily routines and say how often you do things.
Buy things in a shop and ask about prices and sizes.
Order food and drinks in a café or restaurant.
Ask for, give, and follow simple directions.
Talk about the weather and the seasons.
Describe your neighborhood and your town.
Narrate what you did yesterday or last weekend.
Make, accept, and refuse simple invitations.
Talk about future plans with going to.
Compare two places, objects, or people.
steps: Pre-listening: predict the situation from pictures and key vocabulary.
First listening: identify the place, the people, and the purpose.
Second listening: capture prices, times, quantities, and directions.
Third listening: note useful phrases customers and workers use.
Post-listening: answer comprehension questions and order the events.
Extension: role-play a similar conversation with the phrases you heard.
content_json.practiceProduct: Audio comprehension card with situation, key details (price, time, place), useful phrases, and a personal response.
content_json.assessment: Understands the main point and key details (prices, times, places) of short, clear everyday conversations and announcements.

grammarDetails.title: Present simple
grammarDetails.explanation: Describe routines, habits, and facts.

grammarDetails.title: Adverbs of frequency
grammarDetails.explanation: Use always, usually, often, sometimes, and never.

grammarDetails.title: Present continuous
grammarDetails.explanation: Describe actions happening now and near-future plans.

grammarDetails.title: Past simple: regular verbs
grammarDetails.explanation: Add -ed to talk about finished actions.

grammarDetails.title: Past simple: irregular verbs
grammarDetails.explanation: Use went, had, saw, ate, and other common forms.

grammarDetails.title: Past simple: questions and negatives
grammarDetails.explanation: Use did and didn't correctly.

grammarDetails.title: Countable and uncountable nouns
grammarDetails.explanation: Use some, any, much, many, and a lot of.

grammarDetails.title: Comparatives
grammarDetails.explanation: Compare things with -er and more.

grammarDetails.title: Superlatives
grammarDetails.explanation: Use the -est and the most for extremes.

grammarDetails.title: Going to
grammarDetails.explanation: Talk about plans and intentions.

grammarDetails.title: Would like
grammarDetails.explanation: Order food and make polite requests.

grammarDetails.title: Prepositions of time
grammarDetails.explanation: Use in, on, and at with days, months, and hours.

grammarDetails.title: Object pronouns
grammarDetails.explanation: Use me, you, him, her, it, us, and them.

grammarDetails.title: Imperatives for directions
grammarDetails.explanation: Use turn, go, take, and cross to give directions.

title: English B1 · Listening Lab
description: Confident Conversation: Puedo explicar experiencias, opiniones y planes con claridad.

content_json.intro: B1 Listening: follow the main points of clear standard speech on familiar matters such as work, school, travel, and free time. You will practice understanding interviews, podcasts, radio programs, and longer conversations, identifying opinions, reasons, and the speaker's attitude.
content_json.mission: Give your opinion about learning languages and explain two reasons.
content_json.grammar: Present perfect, modals, future forms, connectors.
phrases: The speaker sounds excited / worried / unsure because...

vocabulary.example: In my opinion, practice is essential.
I want to improve my pronunciation.
I study because I need English.
Try to see it from her perspective.
Grammar is still a struggle for her.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.line: You look worried, Nina. What's up?

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: I've been offered a job in another city, and I can't decide what to do.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.line: Wow, congratulations! Have you ever lived away from home?

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: No, never. That's what scares me. Although the salary is better, I'd miss my family.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.line: I understand. If I were you, I'd make a list of advantages and disadvantages.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: That's a good idea. The main advantage is the experience I'd get.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.line: Exactly. And you can always come back if it doesn't work out.

dialogue.speaker: Nina
dialogue.line: True. When I was younger, I used to dream about living in a big city.

dialogue.speaker: Carlos
dialogue.line: Then maybe this is your chance. You should at least visit the city first.

dialogue.speaker: Nina

reading.text: When Sofia decided to spend a semester abroad, many of her friends thought she was making a mistake. She had never traveled alone, she didn't know anyone in Ireland, and her English was far from perfect. However, Sofia believed that the experience would help her grow. The first weeks were harder than she had expected: while she was looking for an apartment, she got lost several times, and she often felt embarrassed when people didn't understand her. Little by little, things improved. She joined a hiking club, made friends from five different countries, and discovered that making mistakes was the fastest way to learn. Now, six months later, Sofia says the semester abroad has been the best decision of her life. Although she still misses her family, she feels more confident and independent than ever. If she had the chance, she would do it all again without hesitation.
questions: What is the main idea of the text?
Why did Sofia's friends think she was making a mistake?
What problems did she have during the first weeks?
What did Sofia discover about making mistakes?
How does she feel now compared to before?
What can we infer about Sofia's personality?
Would you like to study abroad? Explain your opinion with two reasons.

grammarDetails.title: Present perfect
grammarDetails.explanation: Talk about experiences and results.

grammarDetails.title: Present perfect vs past simple
grammarDetails.explanation: Choose between unfinished experience and finished time.

grammarDetails.title: Past continuous
grammarDetails.explanation: Describe background actions in stories.

grammarDetails.title: Past simple vs past continuous
grammarDetails.explanation: Combine interrupted and ongoing past actions.

grammarDetails.title: Will vs going to
grammarDetails.explanation: Choose between predictions, decisions, and plans.

grammarDetails.title: First conditional
grammarDetails.explanation: Talk about real future possibilities.

grammarDetails.title: Second conditional
grammarDetails.explanation: Talk about hypothetical situations.

grammarDetails.title: Should / ought to
grammarDetails.explanation: Give advice and recommendations.

grammarDetails.title: Have to / must
grammarDetails.explanation: Express obligation and necessity.

grammarDetails.title: Comparatives with modifiers
grammarDetails.explanation: Use much, a bit, and far with comparatives.

grammarDetails.title: Relative clauses
grammarDetails.explanation: Add information with who, which, that, and where.

grammarDetails.title: Gerunds and infinitives
grammarDetails.explanation: Use enjoy doing, want to do, and similar patterns.

grammarDetails.title: Used to
grammarDetails.explanation: Describe past habits and states that have changed.

grammarDetails.title: Connectors of contrast and reason
grammarDetails.explanation: Use although, however, because, and therefore.

title: English B2 · Listening Lab
description: Argument and Fluency: Puedo argumentar, comparar y comprender textos más complejos.

content_json.intro: B2 Listening: follow extended speech and complex lines of argument on abstract and concrete topics, including academic lectures, panel discussions, and workplace meetings, provided the topic is reasonably familiar. You will practice identifying the speaker's stance, distinguishing main arguments from supporting examples, and picking up implied meaning and tone.
content_json.mission: Write a short argument about online education.
content_json.grammar: Conditionals, passive voice, reported speech, phrasal verbs.
phrases: This point is illustrated by the example of...
There seems to be a contrast between ... and ...
Could you clarify what you meant by...?

vocabulary.example: Although it is difficult, it is possible.
They have access to good resources.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I've reviewed the proposal, and I have some concerns about the budget.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: I appreciate that. What exactly worries you?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The marketing costs seem high for the results we're projecting.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: That's a fair point, but the data show similar campaigns doubling engagement.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Sure, but our audience is different. Have we considered a smaller pilot first?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Actually, that's a reasonable compromise. It would reduce the risk significantly.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Exactly. And if the pilot works, we can scale up with more confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Priya

reading.text: The debate over remote work has become one of the most contested issues in modern workplaces. Supporters argue that remote work increases productivity, reduces commuting stress, and gives employees greater control over their schedules; several studies have reported measurable gains in output when employees are allowed to choose where they work. Critics, however, point to a decline in spontaneous collaboration, a weakening of company culture, and difficulties in mentoring junior staff remotely. Some companies have responded with hybrid models, requiring employees to be in the office two or three days a week, arguing that this captures the benefits of both approaches. Whether this compromise satisfies either side remains an open question: some employees report that hybrid schedules create the worst of both worlds, with commuting costs but without the full flexibility of remote work. What is clear is that no single policy suits every industry, team, or individual, and organizations that listen carefully to their employees' actual experiences, rather than relying on general assumptions, are more likely to find an arrangement that works.
questions: What is the main argument in favor of remote work?
What are two criticisms mentioned in the text?
What compromise have some companies adopted?
Why might hybrid schedules dissatisfy some employees?
What does the author suggest organizations should do?
Do you think remote work is generally positive or negative? Support your opinion with one reason.

steps: Pre-listening: research the topic and predict likely arguments.

grammarDetails.title: Second conditional
grammarDetails.explanation: Talk about hypothetical situations.

grammarDetails.title: Passive voice
grammarDetails.explanation: Focus on the action or process.

grammarDetails.title: Reported speech
grammarDetails.explanation: Report statements and questions.

grammarDetails.title: Advanced relative clauses
grammarDetails.explanation: Add precise information.

grammarDetails.title: Modals of deduction
grammarDetails.explanation: Use must, might, could, and can't.

grammarDetails.title: Formal linking devices
grammarDetails.explanation: Use nevertheless, furthermore, and in contrast.

grammarDetails.title: Future forms
grammarDetails.explanation: Use will, going to, and present continuous accurately.

grammarDetails.title: Cohesion
grammarDetails.explanation: Use referencing and paragraph unity.

grammarDetails.title: Emphatic structures
grammarDetails.explanation: Use do/does/did for emphasis in statements.

grammarDetails.title: Participle clauses
grammarDetails.explanation: Reduce relative clauses with -ing and -ed forms.

grammarDetails.title: Concession clauses
grammarDetails.explanation: Use even though, despite, and in spite of.

grammarDetails.title: Quantifiers
grammarDetails.explanation: Use a range of quantifiers precisely (few, a few, several, a number of).

grammarDetails.title: Verb patterns
grammarDetails.explanation: Use verb + gerund/infinitive patterns accurately.

grammarDetails.title: Discourse markers
grammarDetails.explanation: Use markers to organize formal spoken and written discourse.

title: English C1 · Listening Lab
description: Advanced Expression: Puedo expresarme con precisión, matices y estilo.

content_json.intro: C1 Listening: understand extended speech even when it is not clearly structured and relationships are only implied, including lectures, debates, and specialized professional discussions delivered at natural speed. You will practice following an argument through digressions, distinguishing the speaker's true position from concessions, and picking up register and tone shifts.
content_json.mission: Rewrite a simple opinion in a more formal academic style.
content_json.grammar: Inversion, advanced passives, hedging, discourse markers.
phrases: The speaker concedes that ..., but ultimately maintains that...
This digression actually reinforces the main point by...
The shift in tone here suggests...
Reading between the lines, the speaker seems to imply...
The underlying assumption behind this claim is...

vocabulary.example: Consider the implication of this choice.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Reyes
dialogue.line: I want to raise a concern about the proposed curriculum change, if I may.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ahmed
dialogue.line: Of course. What's the concern, specifically?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Reyes
dialogue.line: It's not that I oppose the change outright — it's more that the timeline seems unrealistic.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ahmed
dialogue.line: That's a fair concern. What would you consider a more realistic timeline?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Reyes
dialogue.line: At least two semesters, to allow for proper faculty training.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ahmed
dialogue.line: I take your point, though the board has been pushing for faster implementation.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Reyes
dialogue.line: I understand the pressure, but a rushed rollout could undermine the very outcomes we want.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ahmed
dialogue.line: That's a nuance worth raising with the board directly.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Reyes
dialogue.line: I'd be glad to draft a brief memo outlining the risks.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ahmed
dialogue.line: Please do. I suspect it will change the conversation considerably.

reading.text: The idea of meritocracy — that social position should be earned through talent and effort rather than inherited — occupies a peculiar place in contemporary discourse: virtually everyone claims to support it, yet critics argue that its practical effects are often the opposite of what it promises. The central difficulty is that talent and effort are themselves shaped by circumstances largely outside an individual's control: access to education, family stability, and even the confidence to take risks are unevenly distributed long before anyone is old enough to be said to have earned anything. A purely meritocratic system, critics contend, risks converting existing advantages into moral entitlements, so that the successful come to see their position not as fortunate but as deserved, and the unsuccessful internalize failure as a personal shortcoming rather than a structural one. None of this means the underlying ideal — that ability and effort should count for something — is mistaken; it means that any system claiming to reward merit must first reckon honestly with how unevenly the conditions for demonstrating merit are distributed. Absent that reckoning, meritocracy functions less as a corrective to inherited privilege than as its most persuasive disguise.
questions: What tension does the text identify at the heart of meritocracy?
According to the text, what factors shape talent and effort?
What does the text mean by converting 'advantages into moral entitlements'?
What distinction does the author draw between the ideal and the system?
Why does the author call meritocracy potentially 'its most persuasive disguise'?
Do you find the author's argument convincing? Justify your answer with reference to the text.

grammarDetails.title: Advanced clauses
grammarDetails.explanation: Use complex subordination and reduced clauses.

grammarDetails.title: Inversion
grammarDetails.explanation: Use Rarely have we seen...

grammarDetails.title: Cleft sentences
grammarDetails.explanation: Use What matters most is...

grammarDetails.title: Hedging
grammarDetails.explanation: Use may suggest, appears to, and arguably.

grammarDetails.title: Nominalization
grammarDetails.explanation: Use noun forms for academic precision.

grammarDetails.title: Advanced connectors
grammarDetails.explanation: Use notwithstanding, consequently, and in light of.

grammarDetails.title: Mixed conditionals
grammarDetails.explanation: Combine time references.

grammarDetails.title: Register control
grammarDetails.explanation: Adapt language to context.

grammarDetails.title: Subjunctive mood
grammarDetails.explanation: Use the subjunctive in formal recommendations and demands.

grammarDetails.title: Ellipsis
grammarDetails.explanation: Omit repeated elements for concision in formal writing.

grammarDetails.title: Fronting
grammarDetails.explanation: Move elements to the front of the sentence for emphasis.

grammarDetails.title: Collocation precision
grammarDetails.explanation: Choose the exact word that naturally pairs with another.

grammarDetails.title: Discourse cohesion devices
grammarDetails.explanation: Use sophisticated referencing across paragraphs.

grammarDetails.title: Modal nuance
grammarDetails.explanation: Distinguish fine shades of certainty with modal verbs.

title: English C2 · Listening Lab
description: Mastery and Nuance: Puedo comunicarme con naturalidad, precisión y dominio casi nativo.

content_json.intro: C2 Listening: understand any kind of spoken language, live or broadcast, at fast native speed: rapid debate, layered wordplay, dense cultural allusion, dialect, and specialized discourse. You will train real-time critical evaluation of argumentation, detection of manipulation and fallacy, and appreciation of stylistic performance.
content_json.mission: Produce a polished paragraph with nuance and rhetorical balance.
content_json.grammar: Register, idiomatic nuance, rhetorical style, cohesion.
phrases: The speaker's apparent concession is, in fact, a rhetorical trap because...
The allusion to ... reframes the entire exchange...
Beneath the humor lies a pointed critique of...
The fallacy here is a subtle equivocation between...
The pacing and pauses are doing as much work as the words...

vocabulary.example: He has full command of the language.
A skilled speaker can discern subtle tone shifts.
He adapts his register to the audience.
The argument is cohesive and precise.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Your critics say your reform is, and I quote, 'a triumph of packaging over substance.' They have a point, don't they?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: It's a memorable phrase — I'll grant them the packaging. But let's unpack the substance they claim is missing.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Please do. Because the numbers, frankly, are underwhelming.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: Underwhelming compared to what? To the promises of my predecessors, perhaps — promises that, you'll recall, were never kept.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: So your defense is that others failed worse?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: That's your framing, not mine. My claim is narrower and, I'd argue, stronger: modest, verified progress beats grand, imaginary progress.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Some would call that setting the bar on the floor.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: And some would call it standing on solid ground for the first time in a decade. The bar metaphor cuts both ways.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: You're very fond of turning questions around.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: Only the ones that arrive upside down. Shall we look at the actual figures? I brought them — which, you'll notice, is not nothing.

reading.text: It is a truth universally acknowledged — or at least universally repeated, which in our age amounts to much the same thing — that attention has become the scarcest of resources. The phrase 'attention economy' now circulates with the frictionless ease of all successful clichés, and therein lies the difficulty: a metaphor, once ubiquitous, stops being examined. To call attention an economy is to smuggle in a great deal. Economies presuppose ownership, and it is far from clear that I own my attention in the way I own my shoes; it presupposes exchange, though no one has yet explained what I receive for the hours surrendered to the scroll, unless distraction itself is the payment — a currency that devalues on delivery. Above all, the metaphor flatters us with the fiction of rational allocation: the attentive consumer, weighing investments of gaze and interest, when the entire apparatus is engineered, with considerable virtuosity, to ensure that no such weighing occurs. One might object, not unreasonably, that all metaphors mislead, and that this one at least renders a diffuse anxiety discussable. Granted. But there is a difference between a lens and a cage, and the test is whether we can still describe the phenomenon in other terms. Try it: speak of attention for one paragraph without the vocabulary of scarcity, spending, or investment. The difficulty of the exercise is the measure of our captivity — and the beginning, perhaps, of the escape.
questions: How does the opening allusion function, and what does its modification signal?
What three assumptions does the author claim the 'attention economy' metaphor smuggles in?
Explain the irony in the phrase 'a currency that devalues on delivery'.
What objection does the author anticipate, and how is the concession handled?
What distinction does the author draw between 'a lens and a cage'?
What is the function of the imperative 'Try it'? Analyze its rhetorical effect.
Characterize the author's style in three precise terms, with textual evidence for each.
Perform the author's exercise: write a paragraph on attention avoiding all economic vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Nuance"?

Lee en voz alta: The underlying issue is...
¿Qué significa "Discern"?
Produce dos versiones del mismo mensaje en registros distintos (formal e informal).

grammarDetails.title: Full stylistic inversion repertoire
grammarDetails.explanation: Deploy inversion for cadence and climax, not merely correctness.

grammarDetails.title: Syntactic suspension
grammarDetails.explanation: Delay the main clause to build and release tension.

grammarDetails.title: Periodic and loose sentences
grammarDetails.explanation: Alternate sentence architectures for rhythm and emphasis.

grammarDetails.title: Anaphora and parallelism
grammarDetails.explanation: Structure persuasion through deliberate repetition.

grammarDetails.title: Free indirect style
grammarDetails.explanation: Blend narrator and character voice in narrative prose.

grammarDetails.title: Register blending
grammarDetails.explanation: Shift and mix registers for irony, intimacy, or shock.

grammarDetails.title: Archaism and neologism
grammarDetails.explanation: Use old and invented forms with calculated effect.

grammarDetails.title: Elliptical mastery
grammarDetails.explanation: Omit ruthlessly; let structure imply what words need not say.

grammarDetails.title: Punctuation as instrument
grammarDetails.explanation: Command the dash, semicolon, and colon for pacing.

grammarDetails.title: Idiomatic transformation
grammarDetails.explanation: Bend fixed expressions knowingly for wit.

grammarDetails.title: Zeugma and syllepsis
grammarDetails.explanation: Yoke unlike elements to one verb for compression and humor.

grammarDetails.title: Litotes
grammarDetails.explanation: Affirm by negating the contrary: 'not uncommon', 'no small feat'.

grammarDetails.title: Embedded and stacked subordination
grammarDetails.explanation: Control multiply nested clauses without losing the reader.

grammarDetails.title: Prosody in prose
grammarDetails.explanation: Tune stress and syllable patterns so sentences read aloud well.

title: English C2 · Vocabulary Boost

vocabulary.example: He has full command of the language.
A skilled speaker can discern subtle tone shifts.
He adapts his register to the audience.
The argument is cohesive and precise.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Your critics say your reform is, and I quote, 'a triumph of packaging over substance.' They have a point, don't they?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: It's a memorable phrase — I'll grant them the packaging. But let's unpack the substance they claim is missing.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Please do. Because the numbers, frankly, are underwhelming.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: Underwhelming compared to what? To the promises of my predecessors, perhaps — promises that, you'll recall, were never kept.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: So your defense is that others failed worse?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: That's your framing, not mine. My claim is narrower and, I'd argue, stronger: modest, verified progress beats grand, imaginary progress.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: Some would call that setting the bar on the floor.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: And some would call it standing on solid ground for the first time in a decade. The bar metaphor cuts both ways.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: You're very fond of turning questions around.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Vance
dialogue.line: Only the ones that arrive upside down. Shall we look at the actual figures? I brought them — which, you'll notice, is not nothing.

reading.text: It is a truth universally acknowledged — or at least universally repeated, which in our age amounts to much the same thing — that attention has become the scarcest of resources. The phrase 'attention economy' now circulates with the frictionless ease of all successful clichés, and therein lies the difficulty: a metaphor, once ubiquitous, stops being examined. To call attention an economy is to smuggle in a great deal. Economies presuppose ownership, and it is far from clear that I own my attention in the way I own my shoes; it presupposes exchange, though no one has yet explained what I receive for the hours surrendered to the scroll, unless distraction itself is the payment — a currency that devalues on delivery. Above all, the metaphor flatters us with the fiction of rational allocation: the attentive consumer, weighing investments of gaze and interest, when the entire apparatus is engineered, with considerable virtuosity, to ensure that no such weighing occurs. One might object, not unreasonably, that all metaphors mislead, and that this one at least renders a diffuse anxiety discussable. Granted. But there is a difference between a lens and a cage, and the test is whether we can still describe the phenomenon in other terms. Try it: speak of attention for one paragraph without the vocabulary of scarcity, spending, or investment. The difficulty of the exercise is the measure of our captivity — and the beginning, perhaps, of the escape.
questions: How does the opening allusion function, and what does its modification signal?
What three assumptions does the author claim the 'attention economy' metaphor smuggles in?
Explain the irony in the phrase 'a currency that devalues on delivery'.
What objection does the author anticipate, and how is the concession handled?
What distinction does the author draw between 'a lens and a cage'?
What is the function of the imperative 'Try it'? Analyze its rhetorical effect.
Characterize the author's style in three precise terms, with textual evidence for each.
Perform the author's exercise: write a paragraph on attention avoiding all economic vocabulary.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor

dialogue.speaker: Student

reading.text: La educación en línea ha ampliado el acceso al aprendizaje para millones de personas, permitiéndoles estudiar desde cualquier lugar y en cualquier momento. Sin embargo, esta flexibilidad también exige mucha disciplina, una conexión a internet confiable y buenas habilidades digitales, que no todos los estudiantes poseen por igual. Los críticos sostienen que una desventaja importante es la falta de retroalimentación cara a cara, lo cual puede afectar la motivación con el tiempo, sobre todo entre los estudiantes más jóvenes. A pesar de estos desafíos, muchas universidades han invertido en plataformas interactivas pensadas para mantener a los estudiantes comprometidos, usando foros de discusión, sesiones en vivo y retroalimentación personalizada. Como resultado, la tasa de abandono en algunos programas en línea bien diseñados ha comenzado a bajar, lo que sugiere que un diseño de curso cuidadoso puede compensar muchas de las desventajas asociadas normalmente con la educación a distancia.
questions: ¿Qué ha ampliado la educación en línea?
Según los críticos, ¿cuál es una desventaja importante?
¿Qué han hecho algunas universidades para mantener a los estudiantes comprometidos?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "turismo"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: El informe señala que... / Según los residentes...

description: Expresión matizada: Interpreta un ensayo sobre memoria colectiva y lenguaje público.

content_json.intro: Lee un texto denso y separa tesis, ejemplos y conclusión.
content_json.mission: Explica cómo el autor conecta relato, poder y comunidad.
content_json.grammar: Oraciones largas, relativos complejos y puntuación argumentativa.
phrases: La conclusión amplía la tesis inicial.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor

dialogue.speaker: Student

reading.text: Aprender un idioma no es simplemente memorizar formas gramaticales; es una práctica social moldeada por el contexto, la identidad y el propósito. Quien aprende no adquiere reglas gramaticales en el vacío, sino que negocia significado dentro de comunidades específicas, donde la fluidez se juzga tanto por su adecuación como por su corrección. Esta ambigüedad suele derivar de supuestos distintos sobre lo que realmente implica la fluidez en una determinada comunidad discursiva: para algunos significa una pronunciación casi nativa, mientras que para otros es la capacidad de llevar una negociación o defender un argumento de forma persuasiva. Investigaciones recientes en lingüística aplicada sugieren que esta tensión entre corrección y adecuación explica por qué algunos hablantes muy correctos gramaticalmente aún se sienten ajenos en entornos profesionales, mientras que otros, con un control gramatical modesto, logran generar cercanía sin esfuerzo. Reconocer esta distinción puede ayudar a quienes aprenden a fijarse metas más realistas y sensibles al contexto.
questions: ¿Qué no es simplemente aprender un idioma?
Además de la corrección, ¿por qué más se juzga la fluidez?
¿Por qué algunos hablantes muy correctos se sienten ajenos en entornos profesionales?
¿Qué puede ayudar a lograr el reconocer esta distinción?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "relato"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: El autor sostiene que... / A partir de ese ejemplo...

description: Dominio superior: Interpreta una crítica historiográfica con varias capas de sentido.

phrases: El texto presupone un lector experto.
La voz ensayística se vuelve más incisiva.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: El texto presupone un lector experto.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: La voz ensayística se vuelve más incisiva.

reading.text: El verdadero dominio de un idioma implica no solo corrección gramatical, sino también el ritmo, el tono, la conciencia cultural y la capacidad de adaptar el registro a los matices sutiles del contexto. Un hablante verdaderamente cohesivo sabe discernir qué nivel de formalidad conviene a cada intercambio social imprevisible, leyendo entre líneas tanto lo que se dice como, igual de importante, lo que deliberadamente se calla. Este tipo de competencia rara vez se enseña de forma explícita; más bien, se absorbe mediante una exposición prolongada y significativa a los matices culturales y pragmáticos de una lengua, a menudo tras muchos años de inmersión. Paradójicamente, algunos de los hablantes no nativos técnicamente más correctos nunca terminan de alcanzar esta fluidez, precisamente porque su atención permanece fija en la corrección y no en la dinámica interpersonal que ocurre a su alrededor. Por el contrario, quienes aceptan la ambigüedad, toleran el error ocasional y priorizan la conexión por encima de la perfección suelen desarrollar un dominio más intuitivo, casi instintivo, del idioma, uno que ningún libro de gramática podría capturar por completo.
questions: Además de la corrección gramatical, ¿qué cuatro elementos implica el verdadero dominio?
¿Cómo se adquiere normalmente este tipo de competencia?
¿Por qué algunos hablantes técnicamente correctos nunca alcanzan esta fluidez?
¿Qué suelen desarrollar quienes aceptan la ambigüedad?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "alusión"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: El texto presupone un lector experto. / La voz ensayística se vuelve más incisiva.

reading.text: Nadia apprend le français depuis un an et, même si elle fait encore des erreurs, elle se sent plus sûre d'elle quand elle parle avec des touristes. Elle a du mal avec la grammaire, surtout la différence entre le passé composé et l'imparfait, mais sa motivation la pousse à s'entraîner chaque semaine. Depuis qu'elle tient un carnet de vocabulaire, son écriture s'est beaucoup améliorée, et elle a même commencé à lire de courts romans en français. Sa professeure pense que, si elle garde ce rythme, elle pourrait atteindre un niveau intermédiaire avancé d'ici un an.
questions: Depuis combien de temps Nadia apprend-elle le français ?
Quel point de grammaire lui pose des difficultés ?
Qu'est-ce qui l'a aidée à améliorer son écriture ?

reading.text: Le numérique facilite l’accès au savoir, mais il exige aussi autonomie, esprit critique et accompagnement pédagogique. Les critiques estiment qu’un inconvénient majeur est l’absence de retour en face à face, ce qui affecte la motivation avec le temps.
questions: Quel inconvénient majeur mentionnent les critiques ?

title: Français B2 · Reading Quest
description: Débat et argumentation: Puedo defender ideas y comprender opiniones complejas.

content_json.intro: Puedo defender ideas y comprender opiniones complejas.
content_json.mission: Écris un paragraphe sur les avantages du numérique.
content_json.grammar: Subjonctif, discours rapporté, hypothèse, passif.
phrases: Cela s’explique en grande partie par...

vocabulary.example: Ils ont accès à de bonnes ressources.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: D’une part...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: D’autre part...

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: On pourrait avancer que...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Cela s’explique en grande partie par un manque de discipline.

reading.text: L'éducation en ligne a élargi l'accès à l'apprentissage pour des millions de personnes, leur permettant d'étudier n'importe où et à tout moment. Cependant, cette flexibilité exige aussi beaucoup de discipline, une connexion internet fiable et de solides compétences numériques, que tous les étudiants ne possèdent pas de la même façon. Les critiques estiment qu'un inconvénient majeur est l'absence de retour en face à face, ce qui peut affecter la motivation avec le temps, en particulier chez les plus jeunes apprenants. Malgré ces défis, de nombreuses universités ont investi dans des plateformes interactives conçues pour maintenir l'engagement des étudiants, grâce à des forums de discussion, des sessions en direct et un retour personnalisé. Résultat : le taux d'abandon de certains programmes en ligne bien conçus a commencé à baisser, ce qui suggère qu'une conception de cours réfléchie peut compenser bon nombre des inconvénients habituellement associés à l'enseignement à distance.
questions: Qu'est-ce que l'éducation en ligne a élargi ?
Selon les critiques, quel est un inconvénient majeur ?
Qu'ont fait certaines universités pour maintenir l'engagement des étudiants ?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Cependant"?
Écris un paragraphe sur les avantages du numérique.
Lee en voz alta: D’une part...
¿Qué significa "Inconvénient"?
Écris un contre-argument en utilisant "cependant" et "bien que".

title: Français C1 · Reading Quest
description: Expression avancée: Puedo usar registros, matices y estructuras sofisticadas.

content_json.intro: Puedo usar registros, matices y estructuras sofisticadas.
content_json.mission: Reformule une opinion avec un style soutenu.
content_json.grammar: Subjonctif avancé, concession, nominalisation, style.
phrases: Il ne s’agit pas seulement de...
On peut soutenir que le facteur le plus déterminant est...

vocabulary.example: Le discours académique suit des conventions.
La phrase comporte une certaine ambiguïté.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: Il convient de souligner que...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Dans cette perspective...

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: On peut soutenir que le facteur le plus déterminant est...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: C’est précisément cette ambiguïté qui complique la discussion.

reading.text: Apprendre une langue n'est pas simplement mémoriser des formes grammaticales ; c'est une pratique sociale façonnée par le contexte, l'identité et l'intention. Celui qui apprend n'acquiert pas des règles de grammaire dans le vide, mais négocie du sens au sein de communautés spécifiques, où la fluidité est jugée autant sur son adéquation que sur sa correction. Cette ambiguïté provient souvent d'hypothèses différentes sur ce que la fluidité implique réellement dans une communauté discursive donnée : pour certains, cela signifie une prononciation quasi native, tandis que pour d'autres, c'est la capacité à mener une négociation ou à défendre un argument de façon persuasive. Des recherches récentes en linguistique appliquée suggèrent que cette tension entre correction et adéquation explique pourquoi certains locuteurs très corrects sur le plan grammatical se sentent malgré tout étrangers dans un cadre professionnel, tandis que d'autres, avec une maîtrise grammaticale modeste, parviennent à créer un lien sans effort. Reconnaître cette distinction peut aider les apprenants à se fixer des objectifs plus réalistes et sensibles au contexte.
questions: Qu'est-ce qu'apprendre une langue n'est pas simplement ?
Outre la correction, sur quoi la fluidité est-elle aussi jugée ?
Pourquoi certains locuteurs très corrects se sentent-ils étrangers en contexte professionnel ?
Que peut aider à faire la reconnaissance de cette distinction ?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Néanmoins"?
Reformule une opinion avec un style soutenu.
Lee en voz alta: Il convient de souligner que...
¿Qué significa "Cohérence"?
Réécris une phrase simple en utilisant une inversion ou une atténuation (hedging).

title: Français C2 · Reading Quest
description: Maîtrise complète: Puedo expresarme con fineza, precisión y naturalidad.

content_json.intro: Puedo expresarme con fineza, precisión y naturalidad.
content_json.mission: Rédige une réflexion persuasive avec nuance.
content_json.grammar: Rhétorique, ironie, registre, cohésion experte.

vocabulary.example: Un locuteur habile sait discerner les nuances de ton.
Son éloquence a impressionné le public.
Il adapte son registre à son public.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: La question mérite d’être posée...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: À y regarder de près...

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: On pourrait raisonnablement soutenir que...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Loin d’être anodin, ceci reflète une véritable maîtrise du registre.

reading.text: La véritable maîtrise d'une langue implique non seulement la correction grammaticale, mais aussi le rythme, le ton, la conscience culturelle et la capacité à adapter le registre aux nuances subtiles du contexte. Un locuteur véritablement cohérent sait discerner quel niveau de formalité convient à chaque échange social imprévisible, en lisant entre les lignes aussi bien ce qui est dit que, tout aussi important, ce qui est volontairement tu. Ce type de compétence s'enseigne rarement de façon explicite ; elle s'acquiert plutôt par une exposition prolongée et significative aux nuances culturelles et pragmatiques d'une langue, souvent après de nombreuses années d'immersion. Paradoxalement, certains locuteurs non natifs techniquement très corrects n'atteignent jamais tout à fait cette fluidité, précisément parce que leur attention reste fixée sur la correction plutôt que sur la dynamique interpersonnelle qui se joue autour d'eux. À l'inverse, ceux qui acceptent l'ambiguïté, tolèrent l'erreur occasionnelle et privilégient la connexion plutôt que la perfection développent souvent une maîtrise plus intuitive, presque instinctive, de la langue, qu'aucun manuel de grammaire ne pourrait jamais pleinement saisir.
questions: Outre la correction grammaticale, quels quatre éléments implique la véritable maîtrise ?
Comment ce type de compétence s'acquiert-il généralement ?
Pourquoi certains locuteurs techniquement très corrects n'atteignent-ils jamais cette fluidité ?
Que développent souvent ceux qui acceptent l'ambiguïté ?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Éloquent"?
Rédige une réflexion persuasive avec nuance.
Lee en voz alta: La question mérite d’être posée...
¿Qué significa "Discerner"?
Produis deux versions du même message dans des registres différents (formel et informel).

phrases: Si raccomanda di limitare il traffico.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: Il rapporto segnala che...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Secondo i residenti...

reading.text: L'istruzione online ha ampliato l'accesso all'apprendimento per milioni di persone, permettendo loro di studiare ovunque e in qualsiasi momento. Tuttavia, questa flessibilità richiede anche molta disciplina, una connessione internet affidabile e solide competenze digitali, che non tutti gli studenti possiedono allo stesso modo. I critici sostengono che uno svantaggio importante sia la mancanza di un confronto faccia a faccia, il che può influire sulla motivazione nel tempo, specialmente tra gli studenti più giovani. Nonostante queste sfide, molte università hanno investito in piattaforme interattive pensate per mantenere gli studenti coinvolti, usando forum di discussione, sessioni dal vivo e feedback personalizzato. Di conseguenza, il tasso di abbandono in alcuni programmi online ben progettati ha iniziato a diminuire, suggerendo che una progettazione attenta del corso può compensare molti degli svantaggi solitamente associati all'istruzione a distanza.
questions: Secondo i critici, qual è uno svantaggio importante?
Cosa hanno fatto alcune università per mantenere gli studenti coinvolti?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "turismo"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: Il rapporto segnala che... / Secondo i residenti...

reading.text: Imparare una lingua non è semplicemente memorizzare forme grammaticali; è una pratica sociale plasmata dal contesto, dall'identità e dallo scopo. Chi impara non acquisisce regole grammaticali nel vuoto, ma negozia significato all'interno di comunità specifiche, dove la fluidità viene giudicata tanto per la sua adeguatezza quanto per la sua correttezza. Questa ambiguità deriva spesso da presupposti diversi su cosa implichi davvero la fluidità in una determinata comunità discorsiva: per alcuni significa una pronuncia quasi nativa, mentre per altri è la capacità di condurre una trattativa o difendere un argomento in modo persuasivo. Ricerche recenti in linguistica applicata suggeriscono che questa tensione tra correttezza e adeguatezza spiega perché alcuni parlanti molto corretti dal punto di vista grammaticale si sentano comunque estranei in contesti professionali, mentre altri, con un controllo grammaticale modesto, riescano a creare empatia senza sforzo. Riconoscere questa distinzione può aiutare chi impara a fissare obiettivi più realistici e sensibili al contesto.
questions: Cosa non è semplicemente imparare una lingua?
Oltre alla correttezza, per cos'altro viene giudicata la fluidità?
Perché alcuni parlanti molto corretti si sentono estranei in contesti professionali?
Cosa può aiutare a fare il riconoscere questa distinzione?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "racconto"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: L'autore sostiene che... / A partire da questo esempio...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: La voce saggistica diventa più incisiva.

reading.text: La vera padronanza di una lingua implica non solo la correttezza grammaticale, ma anche il ritmo, il tono, la consapevolezza culturale e la capacità di adattare il registro alle sottili sfumature del contesto. Un parlante davvero coeso sa discernere quale livello di formalità si addica a ogni scambio sociale imprevedibile, leggendo tra le righe sia ciò che viene detto sia, altrettanto importante, ciò che viene deliberatamente taciuto. Questo tipo di competenza viene raramente insegnato in modo esplicito; piuttosto, si assorbe attraverso un'esposizione prolungata e significativa alle sfumature culturali e pragmatiche di una lingua, spesso dopo molti anni di immersione. Paradossalmente, alcuni dei parlanti non nativi tecnicamente più corretti non raggiungono mai del tutto questa fluidità, proprio perché la loro attenzione resta fissa sulla correttezza piuttosto che sulle dinamiche interpersonali che si svolgono intorno a loro. Al contrario, chi accetta l'ambiguità, tollera l'errore occasionale e privilegia la connessione rispetto alla perfezione sviluppa spesso una padronanza più intuitiva, quasi istintiva, della lingua, che nessun manuale di grammatica potrebbe mai cogliere pienamente.
questions: Oltre alla correttezza grammaticale, quali quattro elementi implica la vera padronanza?
Come si acquisisce di solito questo tipo di competenza?
Perché alcuni parlanti tecnicamente molto corretti non raggiungono mai questa fluidità?
Cosa sviluppa spesso chi accetta l'ambiguità?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "allusione"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: Il testo presuppone un lettore esperto. / La voce saggistica diventa più incisiva.

reading.text: Online-Bildung hat den Zugang zum Lernen für Millionen von Menschen erweitert und ermöglicht es ihnen, jederzeit und von überall zu lernen. Diese Flexibilität erfordert jedoch auch viel Disziplin, eine zuverlässige Internetverbindung und solide digitale Kompetenzen, die nicht alle Studierenden gleichermaßen besitzen. Kritiker argumentieren, dass ein wesentlicher Nachteil das Fehlen von persönlichem Feedback von Angesicht zu Angesicht sei, was sich mit der Zeit negativ auf die Motivation auswirken kann, besonders bei jüngeren Lernenden. Trotz dieser Herausforderungen haben viele Universitäten in interaktive Plattformen investiert, die Studierende durch Diskussionsforen, Live-Sitzungen und persönliches Feedback stärker einbinden sollen. Infolgedessen ist die Abbrecherquote in einigen gut gestalteten Online-Programmen zurückgegangen, was darauf hindeutet, dass ein durchdachtes Kursdesign viele der üblicherweise mit Fernunterricht verbundenen Nachteile ausgleichen kann.
questions: Was ist laut Kritikern ein wesentlicher Nachteil?

title: Deutsch C1 · Reading Quest
description: Nuancierter Ausdruck: Interpreta un ensayo sobre memoria colectiva y lenguaje público.

content_json.intro: Lee un texto denso y separa tesis, ejemplos y conclusión.
content_json.mission: Explica cómo el autor conecta relato, poder y comunidad.
content_json.grammar: Oraciones largas, relativos complejos y puntuación argumentativa.
phrases: Man sollte die zentrale Metapher beachten.

dialogue.speaker: Tutor
dialogue.line: Der Autor behauptet, dass...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Aus diesem Beispiel ergibt sich...

reading.text: Eine Sprache zu lernen bedeutet nicht bloß, grammatische Formen auswendig zu lernen; es ist eine soziale Praxis, die durch Kontext, Identität und Absicht geprägt wird. Lernende eignen sich Grammatikregeln nicht im luftleeren Raum an, sondern handeln Bedeutung innerhalb bestimmter Gemeinschaften aus, in denen Sprachgewandtheit ebenso nach ihrer Angemessenheit wie nach ihrer Korrektheit beurteilt wird. Diese Mehrdeutigkeit rührt oft von unterschiedlichen Annahmen darüber her, was Sprachgewandtheit in einer bestimmten Diskursgemeinschaft wirklich bedeutet: Für manche heißt das eine nahezu muttersprachliche Aussprache, für andere die Fähigkeit, eine Verhandlung zu führen oder ein Argument überzeugend zu vertreten. Neuere Forschungen in der angewandten Linguistik legen nahe, dass diese Spannung zwischen Korrektheit und Angemessenheit erklärt, warum manche grammatisch sehr korrekte Sprecher sich in beruflichen Situationen dennoch fremd fühlen, während andere mit bescheidener grammatischer Kontrolle mühelos Nähe aufbauen. Das Erkennen dieses Unterschieds kann Lernenden helfen, realistischere, kontextsensiblere Ziele für ihren eigenen Fortschritt zu setzen.
questions: Was bedeutet eine Sprache zu lernen nicht bloß?
Wonach wird Sprachgewandtheit außer nach Korrektheit noch beurteilt?
Warum fühlen sich manche sehr korrekte Sprecher in beruflichen Situationen fremd?
Wozu kann das Erkennen dieses Unterschieds Lernenden verhelfen?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Erzählung"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: Der Autor behauptet, dass... / Aus diesem Beispiel ergibt sich...

dialogue.line: Die essayistische Stimme wird schärfer.

reading.text: Wahre Sprachbeherrschung bedeutet nicht nur grammatische Korrektheit, sondern auch Timing, Tonfall, kulturelles Bewusstsein und die Fähigkeit, das Register an feine Kontextverschiebungen anzupassen. Ein wirklich stimmiger Sprecher erkennt, welches Formalitätsniveau zu jedem unvorhersehbaren sozialen Austausch passt, und liest dabei sowohl zwischen den Zeilen dessen, was gesagt wird, als auch – ebenso wichtig – dessen, was bewusst verschwiegen wird. Diese Art von Kompetenz wird selten explizit gelehrt; vielmehr wird sie durch anhaltende, bedeutungsvolle Auseinandersetzung mit den kulturellen und pragmatischen Nuancen einer Sprache aufgenommen, oft nach vielen Jahren des Eintauchens. Paradoxerweise erreichen manche der technisch korrektesten nicht-muttersprachlichen Sprecher diese Gewandtheit nie ganz, gerade weil ihre Aufmerksamkeit auf die Korrektheit statt auf die zwischenmenschliche Dynamik um sie herum gerichtet bleibt. Umgekehrt entwickeln jene, die Mehrdeutigkeit akzeptieren, gelegentliche Fehler tolerieren und Verbindung über Perfektion stellen, oft eine intuitivere, fast instinktive Beherrschung der Sprache, die kein Grammatikbuch je vollständig erfassen könnte.
questions: Welche vier Dinge umfasst wahre Sprachbeherrschung außer grammatischer Korrektheit?
Wie wird diese Art von Kompetenz normalerweise erworben?
Warum erreichen manche technisch sehr korrekte Sprecher diese Gewandtheit nie ganz?
Was entwickeln oft jene, die Mehrdeutigkeit akzeptieren?

exercises.prompt: ¿Qué significa "Anspielung"?
Lee el texto y resume la idea principal en una frase clara.
Usa al menos dos frases modelo: Der Text setzt einen kundigen Leser voraus. / Die essayistische Stimme wird schärfer.

Kohäsion trägt die Autorität des Textes.

dialogue.line: Nicht nur der Inhalt, auch der Rhythmus ist wichtig.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Der Bezug muss eindeutig bleiben.

title: Hello, Class!
description: A short text about a student's first day of English class, in three parts.

reading.title: Hello, Class!
parts: Hello! My name is Ana. Today is my first English class. I am a little nervous, but also excited. My teacher is Mr. Green. He says, "Good morning, class!" and we answer, "Good morning, Mr. Green!" He has a big smile and a friendly voice.

I sit next to a boy named Leo. "Hi, I'm Leo," he says. "Nice to meet you," I answer. We shake hands and smile. Leo is from Italy, and this is his first day too. We both feel happy to have a new friend.

Mr. Green asks us to say our names and spell them with the alphabet. I spell my name: A-N-A. Leo spells his name too: L-E-O. It is a good first day. I already have one new friend, and I want to learn more English words tomorrow.
questions: What does the class say to Mr. Green?

ordering.prompt: Put the events of the story in order.
events: Ana arrives at her first English class.
Mr. Green says good morning to the class.
Ana meets Leo and they introduce themselves.
Ana and Leo spell their names with the alphabet.

reading.text:
Hello! My name is Ana. Today is my first English class. I am a little nervous, but also excited. My teacher is Mr. Green. He says, "Good morning, class!" and we answer, "Good morning, Mr. Green!" He has a big smile and a friendly voice.

I sit next to a boy named Leo. "Hi, I'm Leo," he says. "Nice to meet you," I answer. We shake hands and smile. Leo is from Italy, and this is his first day too. We both feel happy to have a new friend.

Mr. Green asks us to say our names and spell them with the alphabet. I spell my name: A-N-A. Leo spells his name too: L-E-O. It is a good first day. I already have one new friend, and I want to learn more English words tomorrow.

exercises.prompt: Who is the teacher?
Who does Ana sit next to?
Where is Leo from?
What do they practice at the end?
options: Spelling their names with the alphabet

exercises.prompt: True or false: Ana is a little nervous on her first day.
True or false: Leo is Ana's teacher.
True or false: Ana and Leo shake hands.
Choose the best word: Mr. Green has a big smile and a very ___ voice.

title: Nice to Meet You
description: Listen to two students meeting for the first time.

content_json.intro: Listen to Ana and Leo meeting on the first day of class. Focus on how they say hello and introduce themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: Hello! My name is Ana. What's your name?
dialogue.translation: Hola, me llamo Ana. ¿Cómo te llamas?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: Hi, Ana! I'm Leo. Nice to meet you.
dialogue.translation: Hola, Ana. Soy Leo. Mucho gusto.

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: Nice to meet you too. How are you?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: I'm fine, thanks. And you?

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: Good morning! I mean... I'm fine too!

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: See you later, Ana!

exercises.prompt: What does Leo say when he meets Ana?
How does Ana say goodbye at the end?
What does Leo answer to "How are you?"

title: Say Hello
description: Practice greeting someone and introducing yourself out loud.

content_json.mission: Say hello to a partner, introduce yourself with your name, and ask how they are.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Hello! My name is...

dialogue.speaker: Partner
dialogue.line: Hi! I'm... Nice to meet you.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Nice to meet you too. How are you?

dialogue.speaker: Partner
dialogue.line: I'm fine, thank you. And you?

exercises.prompt: Say out loud: "Hello, my name is..." and finish the sentence with your own name.
Practice the greeting dialogue with a partner, then switch roles.

title: Write Your Introduction
description: Write three simple sentences introducing yourself.

content_json.mission: Write 3 sentences: your name, how you are today, and one thing you say hello with (e.g. a friend's name).

dialogue.speaker: Model
dialogue.line: My name is Ana. I am fine today. Hello, Leo!
dialogue.translation: Me llamo Ana. Estoy bien hoy. ¡Hola, Leo!

exercises.prompt: Write 3 short sentences to introduce yourself, using "My name is...", "I am...", and "Hello, ...!"

title: The Verb "To Be": am / is / are
description: Learn how to use am, is and are with I, you, he/she/it, we, they.

content_json.grammar: The verb "to be" changes depending on the subject: I am, you are, he/she/it is, we are, they are. We use it to say names, feelings, and where we are from. Example: "I am Ana. You are my friend. He is the teacher."

exercises.prompt: I ___ a student.
She ___ my teacher.
They ___ my classmates.
You ___ very kind.

title: Greeting Words
description: Key words for saying hello and introducing yourself.

exercises.prompt: What does "Hello" mean?
What does "Nice to meet you" mean?
What does "Teacher" mean?

title: My Profile
description: A short profile text about a student from the Dominican Republic, in three parts.

reading.title: My Profile
parts: My name is Marco. I am twelve years old. I am from the Dominican Republic, and I live in Santo Domingo. My city is big and colorful, with many parks and friendly people. I go to a school near my house with my sister.
Today is a special day for me: it is my birthday! I am very happy today. I am not tired, and I am not sad at all. My friends say "Happy birthday!" to me at school, and I feel very lucky.

I am a student, and I am also a good friend. I like to help my classmates with their homework. My favorite color is blue, like the sky. Tonight, my family is going to have a small party for my birthday.

ordering.prompt: Put the events of the story in order.
events: Marco wakes up on his birthday.
His friends say "Happy birthday" at school.
Marco helps a classmate with homework.
Marco's family has a small party.

reading.text:
My name is Marco. I am twelve years old. I am from the Dominican Republic, and I live in Santo Domingo. My city is big and colorful, with many parks and friendly people. I go to a school near my house with my sister.

Today is a special day for me: it is my birthday! I am very happy today. I am not tired, and I am not sad at all. My friends say "Happy birthday!" to me at school, and I feel very lucky.

I am a student, and I am also a good friend. I like to help my classmates with their homework. My favorite color is blue, like the sky. Tonight, my family is going to have a small party for my birthday.

exercises.prompt: How old is Marco?
Where does Marco live?
Why is Marco happy?
Who does Marco help at school?
True or false: Marco is tired today.
True or false: Marco's favorite color is blue.
True or false: Marco's family is having a party tonight.
Choose the best word: My city is big and ___, with many parks.

title: Where Are You From?
description: Listen to two students talking about their age and country.

content_json.intro: Listen to Sofia and Leo talking about where they are from and how old they are.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: Where are you from, Leo?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: I am from Italy. And you?

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: I am from the Dominican Republic. How old are you?
dialogue.translation: Soy de República Dominicana. ¿Cuántos años tienes?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: I am twelve years old. What about you?

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: I am eleven. Are you happy at this school?
dialogue.translation: Tengo once. ¿Estás feliz en esta escuela?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: Yes, I am very happy here!

exercises.prompt: Where is Leo from?
How old is Sofia?
Is Leo happy at the school?

title: Talk About Yourself
description: Practice saying your age, country and how you feel today.

content_json.mission: Tell a partner your name, your age, where you are from, and how you feel today.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I am ... years old and I am from...

dialogue.speaker: Partner
dialogue.line: Nice! How do you feel today?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: Today I am happy because...

exercises.prompt: Say out loud: your age, your country, and one feeling you have today.
Ask a partner "How old are you?" and "Where are you from?" and answer their questions too.

title: Write a Mini Profile
description: Write four sentences about yourself: name, age, country, feeling.

content_json.mission: Write 4 sentences about yourself using "My name is...", "I am ... years old", "I am from...", and "Today I am...".

dialogue.speaker: Model
dialogue.line: My name is Marco. I am twelve years old. I am from the Dominican Republic. Today I am happy.
dialogue.translation: Me llamo Marco. Tengo doce años. Soy de República Dominicana. Hoy estoy feliz.

exercises.prompt: Write a 4-sentence mini profile about yourself (name, age, country, feeling today).

title: To Be: Negative and Questions
description: Learn "I am not...", "Is he...?" and possessive adjectives my/your.

content_json.grammar: To make "to be" negative, add "not": I am not tired. She is not sad. To ask a question, put the verb first: Are you happy? Is he from Spain? We also use "my" (mine) and "your" (yours) before a noun: my name, your country.

exercises.prompt: I ___ tired today. (negative)
___ you from Italy?
___ she happy today?
What is ___ name? (talking to a friend)

title: Feelings and Countries
description: Words to describe age, countries and feelings.

exercises.prompt: What does "Happy" mean?
What does "Tired" mean?
What does "Country" mean?

title: My Family
description: A short text about Ana's family members, in three parts.

reading.title: My Family
parts: This is my family. My mother's name is Carmen, and my father's name is Julio. My parents work near our house, and they always have dinner with us. We live in a small house with a big garden.

I have one sister and one brother. My sister's name is Sofia; she is sixteen years old, and she loves music. My brother's name is Marco; he is nine, and he loves soccer. We play together every weekend.

My grandmother lives with us too. Her name is Rosa, and she is very kind. She cooks delicious food for the family. On Sundays, we all eat lunch together and talk about our week. We are a happy family.
questions: How many brothers and sisters does the writer have?
Who lives with the family besides the parents?

ordering.prompt: Put the events of the story in order.
events: Carmen and Julio have dinner with the family.
Sofia and Marco play together on the weekend.
Rosa cooks lunch on Sunday.
The family eats lunch together and talks.

reading.text:
This is my family. My mother's name is Carmen, and my father's name is Julio. My parents work near our house, and they always have dinner with us. We live in a small house with a big garden.

I have one sister and one brother. My sister's name is Sofia; she is sixteen years old, and she loves music. My brother's name is Marco; he is nine, and he loves soccer. We play together every weekend.

My grandmother lives with us too. Her name is Rosa, and she is very kind. She cooks delicious food for the family. On Sundays, we all eat lunch together and talk about our week. We are a happy family.

exercises.prompt: What is the father's name?
How old is the sister?
Who is Rosa?
What does Marco love?
True or false: Sofia loves music.
True or false: The family lives in a big city apartment.
True or false: Rosa cooks for the family.
Choose the best word: Rosa is very ___; she cooks delicious food for us.

title: This Is My Brother
description: Listen to Ana introducing her brother to a friend.

content_json.intro: Listen to Ana showing a photo of her family to her friend Leo and introducing her brother.

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: Is this your family, Ana?

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: Yes! This is my mother and this is my father.
dialogue.translation: Sí, esta es mi madre y este es mi padre.

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: And who is this boy?

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: That's my brother, Marco. He is nine years old.
dialogue.translation: Ese es mi hermano, Marco. Tiene nueve años.

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.line: Do you have a sister too?

dialogue.speaker: Ana
dialogue.line: Yes, my sister's name is Sofia.
dialogue.translation: Sí, mi hermana se llama Sofia.

exercises.prompt: What is the brother's name?
How old is the brother?
What does Ana have besides a brother?

title: Describe Your Family
description: Practice talking about the people in your family.

content_json.mission: Tell a partner about two people in your family: who they are and one thing about them.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: I have a sister. Her name is...

dialogue.speaker: Partner
dialogue.line: How old is she?

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: She is ... years old.

exercises.prompt: Say out loud two sentences about a family member (name and age or one detail).
Ask a partner "Do you have a sister or brother?" and listen to their answer.

title: Write About Your Family
description: Write a short paragraph describing your family.

content_json.mission: Write 3-4 sentences describing your family: who is in it and one detail about each person.

dialogue.speaker: Model
dialogue.line: This is my family. I have one sister. My sister's name is Sofia. She is sixteen years old.
dialogue.translation: Esta es mi familia. Tengo una hermana. Mi hermana se llama Sofia. Tiene dieciséis años.

exercises.prompt: Write 3-4 sentences describing your family members and one detail about each.

title: Possessive 's and Plural Nouns
description: Learn how to show who something belongs to and how to make plurals.

content_json.grammar: We add 's to a name or person to show possession: Ana's brother, my sister's name. To make most nouns plural, add -s: one brother → two brothers, one friend → three friends. Some plurals are irregular: one child → two children.

exercises.prompt: How do you say the name belonging to Ana's mother?
What is the plural of "friend"?
What is the plural of "child"?
Choose the correct sentence.

title: Family Members
description: Words for the people in your family.

vocabulary.example: My father works at a school.
My sister is sixteen years old.

exercises.prompt: What does "Brother" mean?
What does "Grandmother" mean?
What does "Cousin" mean?

title: My Classroom
description: A short text describing a classroom and school subjects, in three parts.

reading.title: My Classroom
parts: My classroom is big and bright. There is a whiteboard, a teacher's desk, and twenty student desks. There are books, pencils, and a big clock on the wall. The walls are yellow, and there are colorful posters everywhere.

My favorite subject is English, but I also like Math and Art. In English class, we learn new words and sing songs. In Art class, we draw and paint pictures. Math is difficult sometimes, but my teacher helps me.

There isn't a computer in my classroom, but there is a computer lab at school. We go there once a week to use computers. I like my school very much because my teachers are kind and my friends are fun.
questions: What is on the wall of the classroom?
What is the writer's favorite subject?

ordering.prompt: Put the events of the story in order.
events: Students sit at their desks in the classroom.
The class studies English and sings songs.
The class studies Art and paints pictures.
The class visits the computer lab.

reading.text:
My classroom is big and bright. There is a whiteboard, a teacher's desk, and twenty student desks. There are books, pencils, and a big clock on the wall. The walls are yellow, and there are colorful posters everywhere.

My favorite subject is English, but I also like Math and Art. In English class, we learn new words and sing songs. In Art class, we draw and paint pictures. Math is difficult sometimes, but my teacher helps me.

There isn't a computer in my classroom, but there is a computer lab at school. We go there once a week to use computers. I like my school very much because my teachers are kind and my friends are fun.

exercises.prompt: What is on the wall?
What is the favorite subject?
Where is the computer?
What do they do in Art class?
True or false: The classroom walls are yellow.
True or false: There is a computer in the classroom.
True or false: Math is always easy for the writer.
Choose the best word: The classroom is big and ___, with yellow walls.

title: What's in Your Bag?
description: Listen to two classmates checking their school bags.

content_json.intro: Listen to Sofia and Marco talking about what is in their school bags before class.
phrases: Is there a... in your bag?

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: Do you have a pencil, Marco?

dialogue.speaker: Marco
dialogue.line: Yes, I have two pencils and a pen.
dialogue.translation: Sí, tengo dos lápices y un bolígrafo.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: Great, can I borrow one, please?

dialogue.speaker: Marco
dialogue.line: Sure, here you are.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.line: Thank you! Is there a notebook in your bag too?
dialogue.translation: ¡Gracias! ¿Hay también un cuaderno en tu mochila?

dialogue.speaker: Marco
dialogue.line: Yes, there is one notebook and one book.
dialogue.translation: Sí, hay un cuaderno y un libro.

exercises.prompt: How many pencils does Marco have?
What does Sofia ask to borrow?
What else is in Marco's bag?

title: What's in Your Bag?
description: Practice asking and answering about school objects.

content_json.mission: Ask a partner what is in their school bag and answer about your own bag.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: What is in your bag?

dialogue.speaker: Partner
dialogue.line: There is a notebook and two pens.
dialogue.translation: Hay un cuaderno y dos bolígrafos.

dialogue.speaker: You
dialogue.line: What is your favorite subject?

exercises.prompt: Say out loud three school objects that are in your bag.
Ask a partner "What is your favorite subject?" and tell them yours.

title: Write About Your Classroom
description: Write sentences describing your classroom and favorite subject.

content_json.mission: Write 3-4 sentences describing your classroom (what is in it) and your favorite subject.

dialogue.speaker: Model
dialogue.line: There is a whiteboard and there are twenty desks in my classroom. My favorite subject is English.
dialogue.translation: Hay una pizarra y hay veinte pupitres en mi salón. Mi materia favorita es inglés.

exercises.prompt: Write 3-4 sentences about your classroom and your favorite subject, using "There is/There are".

title: There Is / There Are + Articles
description: Learn to say what exists using there is / there are, and a / an / the.

content_json.grammar: We use "there is" with singular nouns and "there are" with plural nouns: There is a clock. There are twenty desks. Use "a" before a consonant sound (a pen) and "an" before a vowel sound (an eraser). Use "the" when both speakers know exactly which thing we mean: the whiteboard (the one in our classroom).

exercises.prompt: ___ a computer lab at school.
___ twenty students in my class.
I have ___ eraser in my bag.
I have ___ pencil.

title: School Objects and Subjects
description: Words for classroom objects and school subjects.

vocabulary.example: The teacher writes on the whiteboard.
I do my homework after school.

exercises.prompt: What does "Notebook" mean?
What does "Subject" mean?
What does "Homework" mean?

title: A Normal Day
description: A short text about Ana's daily routine, in three parts.

reading.title: A Normal Day
parts: Every day, I get up at seven o'clock. First, I brush my teeth and wash my face. Then I eat breakfast with my family: bread, eggs, and juice. After breakfast, I walk to school with my friend Sofia. We talk about our homework on the way.

School starts at eight o'clock. I have English, Math, and Science in the morning. At noon, I eat lunch with my classmates in the cafeteria. In the afternoon, I do my homework at home, and then I play soccer with my brother in the garden.

In the evening, my family has dinner together at seven o'clock. We talk about our day and laugh a lot. After dinner, I read a book for a little while. I go to bed at nine thirty. I always sleep well because my day is full of activities!

Every day, I get up at seven o'clock. First, I brush my teeth and wash my face. Then I eat breakfast with my family: bread, eggs, and juice. After breakfast, I walk to school with my friend Sofia. We talk about our homework on the way.

School starts at eight o'clock. I have English, Math, and Science in the morning. At noon, I eat lunch with my classmates in the cafeteria. In the afternoon, I do my homework at home, and then I play soccer with my brother in the garden.

In the evening, my family has dinner together at seven o'clock. We talk about our day and laugh a lot. After dinner, I read a book for a little while. I go to bed at nine thirty. I always sleep well because my day is full of activities!

title: My Weekly Schedule
description: A short text about a student's weekly schedule, in three parts.

reading.title: My Weekly Schedule
parts: My English class is on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, at nine o'clock in the morning. I also have Math on Tuesday and Thursday, at ten o'clock. My classes finish at two o'clock in the afternoon, and then I go home for lunch.

My favorite day is Saturday because I don't have school. On Saturday mornings, I usually clean my room and help my mother. In the afternoon, I play with my friends in the park. Sunday is a quiet day; my family rests and visits my grandmother.

My birthday is in June, on the fifteenth. This year, my birthday is on a Sunday, so I can celebrate all day with my family! In the summer, in July and August, we don't have classes at all. I love summer because I can sleep late and travel.

My English class is on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, at nine o'clock in the morning. I also have Math on Tuesday and Thursday, at ten o'clock. My classes finish at two o'clock in the afternoon, and then I go home for lunch.

My favorite day is Saturday because I don't have school. On Saturday mornings, I usually clean my room and help my mother. In the afternoon, I play with my friends in the park. Sunday is a quiet day; my family rests and visits my grandmother.

My birthday is in June, on the fifteenth. This year, my birthday is on a Sunday, so I can celebrate all day with my family! In the summer, in July and August, we don't have classes at all. I love summer because I can sleep late and travel.

title: Prepositions of Time: at / on / in
description: Learn when to use at, on and in with times, days and months.

content_json.grammar: Use "at" with exact times: at seven o'clock, at noon. Use "on" with days and dates: on Monday, on June 15th. Use "in" with months, years and longer periods: in June, in the morning, in summer. Questions with these use "What time...?" and "What day...?".

title: Lunch at School
description: A short text about what a student eats for lunch, in three parts.

reading.title: Lunch at School
parts: At lunchtime, I usually eat rice, chicken, and some vegetables. My mother cooks a big lunch for the whole family every day. We eat together at the table and talk about our morning. Lunch is my favorite meal of the day.

I love fruit, especially bananas and mangoes. I eat fruit every afternoon as a snack. I don't like fish very much, but my brother loves it. He eats fish two or three times a week. We don't always like the same food!

For drinks, I have some water or juice with my meals. I don't drink coffee because it's only for adults. On Fridays, we sometimes have pizza for dinner, and everybody is happy! Pizza with cheese is my favorite kind.
